_note: Vendored from brooks-lint skills/_shared/decay-risks.md (R1-R6) + test-decay-risks.md (T1-T6). Single source for every risk definition the scanners + skills read (CLAUDE.md rule 2). The `decidable` array names the existing analyze rule-ids that mechanically evidence each risk (Spec 354 §3); empty = judgment-only. The 12-book matrix is owned by Spec 358 source-coverage.json; books here use the same canonical titles. Drift-tracked: scripts/check-drift compares `_source` against the pinned upstream rev (Spec 354 §2).

R1.name: Cognitive Overload
R1.diagnostic: How much mental effort does a human need to understand this?
R1.consequence: Cognitive load beyond working memory causes mistakes, avoidance, and blocks the refactoring that would fix it.
R1.remedy: Extract cohesive blocks into well-named functions, flatten nesting with guard clauses, and replace magic values and cryptic names with intention-revealing ones (Fowler — Extract Function; McConnell — High-Quality Routines).
symptoms: Function longer than 20 lines where multiple levels of abstraction are mixed together
Nesting depth greater than 3 levels
Parameter list with more than 4 parameters
Magic numbers or unexplained constants
Variable names that require reading the implementation to understand (e.g. d, tmp2, flag)
Boolean expressions with 3 or more conditions combined
Train-wreck chains: a.getB().getC().doD()
Code names that do not match what the business calls the same concept
Flag Arguments: a boolean parameter that makes the function do two fundamentally different things
Primitive Obsession: domain concepts represented as primitive types rather than purpose-built value types
Shallow module: the interface is more complex relative to the functionality it provides

sources.principle: Ch. 11: The Power of Variable Names

sources.book: Ousterhout — A Philosophy of Software Design

severity_guide.critical: function > 50 lines, nesting > 5, or virtually no meaningful names
severity_guide.warning: function 20-50 lines, nesting 4-5, some unclear names
severity_guide.suggestion: minor naming issues, 1-2 magic numbers, isolated train-wreck chains

what_not_to_flag: Linear code with clear names and guard clauses is not automatically high cognitive load
Internal implementation detail hidden behind a deep, simple module boundary is not a shallow-module problem
Domain-specific terminology should not be flagged if it matches how experts actually speak

R2.name: Change Propagation
R2.diagnostic: How many unrelated things break when you change one thing?
R2.consequence: Each change ripples to unrelated modules, slowing velocity and multiplying regression risk.
R2.remedy: Localise the decision: gather the divergent reasons-to-change into one module and depend on abstractions rather than concretions, so a change has one home (Martin — DIP; Fowler — Move Method).
symptoms: Modifying one feature requires touching more than 3 files in unrelated modules
One class changes for multiple different business reasons (Divergent Change)
A method uses more data from another class than from its own class (Feature Envy)
Two classes know each other's internal state directly (Inappropriate Intimacy)
Changing one module requires recompiling or retesting many unrelated modules
Hyrum's Law: with enough callers, every observable behavior becomes an implicit contract
Orthogonality violation: changing one dimension forces edits in unrelated dimensions
Information Leakage: a design decision is encoded in more than one module

sources.book: Winters et al. — Software Engineering at Google
Ousterhout — A Philosophy of Software Design

severity_guide.critical: one change touches > 5 files, or there is a structural dependency inversion (domain depends on infrastructure)
severity_guide.warning: one change touches 3-5 files, mild coupling between modules

what_not_to_flag: A composition root wiring concrete dependencies is not a DIP violation by itself
A stable public API with intentionally supported behavior is not automatically Hyrum's Law debt
Similar edits inside one bounded context may be normal coordinated change, not shotgun surgery

R3.name: Knowledge Duplication
R3.diagnostic: Is the same decision expressed in more than one place?
R3.consequence: Multiple copies drift apart silently — DRY is about decisions, not code lines.
R3.remedy: Extract the duplicated decision into a single source of truth and have every site reference it (Hunt & Thomas — DRY; Fowler — Extract Function/Class).
symptoms: Same logic copy-pasted across multiple files or functions
Same concept named differently in different parts of the codebase (user/account/member/customer)
Parallel class hierarchies that must change in sync
Configuration values repeated as literals in multiple places
Two modules that implement the same algorithm independently

severity_guide.critical: core business logic duplicated across modules, or same domain concept named 3+ different ways
severity_guide.warning: utility code duplicated, naming inconsistent within a subsystem
severity_guide.suggestion: minor literal duplication, single naming inconsistency

what_not_to_flag: Repetition across separate bounded contexts is not automatically duplicate knowledge
Temporary duplication during an active extraction or migration is not necessarily debt
Shared protocol constants repeated at explicit boundaries may be acceptable when local ownership is clearer

R4.name: Accidental Complexity
R4.diagnostic: Is the code more complex than the problem it solves?
R4.consequence: Accidental complexity accumulates addition by addition until developers fight scaffolding more than solving the problem.
R4.remedy: Delete the speculative abstraction and inline lazy classes and middle-men until a real second consumer appears (Fowler — Inline Function/Class; YAGNI).
symptoms: Abstractions built for future use with no current consumer (Speculative Generality)
Classes that barely justify their existence — wrap a single method call (Lazy Class)
Classes that only delegate to another class without adding behavior (Middle Man)
Second attempt at a system significantly more elaborate than the first (Second System Effect)
Switch statements that signal missing polymorphism
Configuration options that have never been changed from their defaults
Framework code larger than the application it powers
Code grown under sustained tactical shortcuts

sources.book: Ousterhout — A Philosophy of Software Design

severity_guide.critical: an entire subsystem built around a speculative requirement, or framework overhead dominates domain logic
severity_guide.warning: several unnecessary abstractions or wrapper classes, unused configuration systems
severity_guide.suggestion: one or two lazy classes or middle-man patterns in non-critical paths

what_not_to_flag: A switch over an external protocol, wire format, or closed enum is not automatically missing polymorphism
Thin wrappers that absorb vendor churn or hide instability may be justified
A larger second version is not second-system effect unless the added generality exceeds present needs

R5.name: Dependency Disorder
R5.diagnostic: Do dependencies flow in a consistent, predictable direction?
R5.consequence: When business logic depends on infrastructure, infrastructure changes cascade into domain changes, and cycles prevent isolation.
R5.remedy: Break the cycle by inverting the dependency behind an interface and pointing it toward stable abstractions; keep fan-out within a single layer (Martin — ADP/DIP/SDP).
symptoms: Circular dependencies between modules or packages
High-level business logic directly imports from low-level infrastructure
Stable, widely-used components depend on unstable, frequently-changing ones
Abstract components depending on concrete implementations
Law of Demeter violations: order.getCustomer().getAddress().getCity()
Module fan-out greater than 5 (imports from more than 5 other modules)
A module implements an interface but uses only a subset of its methods (ISP violation)
Different modules use incompatible architectural patterns with no clear rule
Direct version-pinned dependencies on transitive packages (diamond dependency risk)

sources.principle: Ch. 5: Decoupling and the Law of Demeter

sources.book: Winters et al. — Software Engineering at Google

severity_guide.critical: dependency cycles present, or domain layer directly depends on infrastructure layer
severity_guide.warning: several SDP or DIP violations but no cycles; conceptual inconsistency across modules
severity_guide.suggestion: minor Demeter violations, slightly elevated fan-out in isolated modules

what_not_to_flag: High fan-out in an orchestration layer or composition root is not automatically disorder
Adapter modules may depend on both domain and infrastructure when they explicitly translate across the boundary
A stable facade over many leaf dependencies can be healthy if dependency policy is clear

R6.name: Domain Model Distortion
R6.diagnostic: Does the code faithfully represent the problem it is solving?
R6.consequence: Code that mismatches business language forces mental translation; over time it models schemas instead of the domain, with logic bleeding into service layers.
R6.remedy: Move behaviour onto the domain objects and align names with the ubiquitous language, adding an anti-corruption layer at context boundaries (Evans — Domain Model / Ubiquitous Language).
symptoms: Business logic scattered across service layers while domain objects have only getters and setters (anemic domain model)
Code names that do not match what business stakeholders call the concept
A class whose only purpose is to hold data with no behavior (pure data bag)
A subclass that ignores or overrides most of its parent's behavior (Refused Bequest)
Bounded context boundaries crossed without any translation or anti-corruption layer
Methods more interested in the data of another class than their own (Feature Envy)
LSP violation: a subclass overrides parent methods with incompatible behavior
Value Objects treated as Entities: a concept defined by its attributes given a mutable ID and lifecycle

severity_guide.critical: domain logic entirely in service layer, domain objects are pure data bags with no behavior
severity_guide.warning: partial anemia, some naming inconsistency between code and domain language
severity_guide.suggestion: minor naming drift in non-core areas, isolated cases of Feature Envy

what_not_to_flag: CRUD-heavy workflows may legitimately use transaction scripts instead of rich domain objects
DTOs, persistence records, and API payload models are allowed to be data-only
Shared infrastructure language should not be mistaken for domain drift if the business model itself is simple

T1.name: Test Obscurity
T1.diagnostic: How much effort does it take to understand what this test verifies?
T1.consequence: Unclear test intent breeds distrust, missed failures, and duplicates — one step from an abandoned suite.
T1.remedy: Name each test by scenario and expected outcome, inline the mystery guest into the test body, and give every assertion a message (Osherove — naming; Meszaros).
symptoms: Assertion Roulette: multiple assertions with no message string
Mystery Guest: test depends on external state not visible in the test body
Test names that do not express the scenario and expected outcome (test1, shouldWork)
General Fixture: an oversized setUp shared by unrelated tests
Test body requires reading production code to understand what is being verified

sources.book: Osherove — The Art of Unit Testing

severity_guide.critical: no test name in the file describes the behavior being tested; all assertions lack messages
severity_guide.warning: multiple Mystery Guests; several ambiguous test names
severity_guide.suggestion: minor naming issues; isolated General Fixture

what_not_to_flag: Multiple assertions are acceptable when they describe one coherent behavior and fail with a clear story
Shared setup is fine when every initialized value is relevant to nearly every test
Concise test names are acceptable if scenario and expected outcome are still obvious

T2.name: Test Brittleness
T2.diagnostic: Do tests break when you refactor without changing behavior?
T2.consequence: Brittle tests punish refactoring — eventually developers stop refactoring and the codebase stagnates to protect the suite.
T2.remedy: Assert observable behaviour rather than implementation, and split eager tests so a single behavior change fails a single test (Meszaros; Osherove — test isolation).
symptoms: Tests assert on private method results or internal state rather than observable behavior
Eager Test: one test method verifies multiple unrelated behaviors
Over-specified: assertions enforce mock call order or exact parameter values irrelevant to the behavior
Renaming or extracting a method causes 5 or more tests to fail though no behavior changed
Erratic Test: produces different results across runs without production changes

sources.book: Osherove — The Art of Unit Testing

severity_guide.critical: refactoring with no behavior change causes test failures; > 5 tests coupled to a single implementation detail
severity_guide.warning: Eager Tests common across the suite; moderate implementation-detail assertions

what_not_to_flag: Verifying an externally observable event or emitted command is not implementation coupling
One test with several assertions is acceptable when all assertions support one behavior claim
A fake or in-memory adapter is not brittleness if the test still asserts behavior, not wiring

T3.name: Test Duplication
T3.diagnostic: Is the same test scenario expressed in more than one place?
T3.consequence: Duplicated tests must change in multiple places and create false confidence without testing distinct behavior.
T3.remedy: Extract shared setup and assertions into named helpers, and delete lazy duplicate tests that verify no distinct behavior (Meszaros — Test Code Duplication; DRY).
symptoms: Test Code Duplication: same setup or assertion logic copy-pasted across multiple tests
Lazy Test: multiple tests verifying identical behavior with no differentiation
Same boundary condition tested identically at unit, integration, and E2E level
Test helper functions or fixtures duplicated across test files instead of shared

severity_guide.critical: core business scenario fully duplicated across all three test layers with no differentiation
severity_guide.warning: common scenario setup repeated in 5 or more tests without extraction
severity_guide.suggestion: minor helper duplication; isolated Lazy Tests

what_not_to_flag: The same scenario may appear at unit and integration level when each layer verifies a distinct risk
Small local setup duplication can be clearer than an over-abstracted fixture maze
Similar assertions against different domain rules are not Lazy Tests if the business intent differs

T4.name: Mock Abuse
T4.diagnostic: Is the test more complex than the behavior it tests?
T4.consequence: Mock abuse produces tests that pass while verifying nothing — production code can be fully broken as long as the mocks are wired up.
T4.remedy: Replace mock-interaction assertions with state and behaviour checks, cap mocks per test, and remove test-only methods from production classes (Osherove; Meszaros — Behavior Verification).
symptoms: Mock setup code is longer than the test logic itself
Primary assertion is expect(mock).toHaveBeenCalledWith(...) — verifies a call, not behavior
Test-only methods added to production classes for lifecycle management in tests
Single unit test uses more than 3 mocks
Incomplete Mock: mock missing fields downstream code will access
Hard-Coded Test Data: test data has no resemblance to real data shapes or constraints

sources.book: Osherove — The Art of Unit Testing
Feathers — Working Effectively with Legacy Code
Osherove — The Art of Unit Testing

severity_guide.critical: mock setup > 50% of test code; production class has methods only called from tests
severity_guide.warning: mocks consistently > 3 per test; primary assertions are mock call verifications
severity_guide.suggestion: isolated Incomplete Mocks; minor Hard-Coded Test Data

what_not_to_flag: A small number of mocks around nondeterministic dependencies is acceptable when assertions still verify behavior
Fakes and spies used to observe state transitions are not mock abuse by default
One interaction assertion may be appropriate when the interaction itself is the behavior under test

T5.name: Coverage Illusion
T5.diagnostic: Does the test suite actually protect against the failures that matter?
T5.consequence: Coverage measures execution, not verification; 90% line coverage can still miss every critical failure mode.
T5.remedy: Add tests for the change paths that matter — sad paths, null/empty/boundary inputs, and important side effects — rather than chasing a line-coverage number (Feathers; Google — change coverage).
symptoms: High line coverage but error-handling branches, boundary conditions, and exception paths have no tests
Happy-path only: no sad paths, no null/empty/zero inputs, no concurrency edge cases
Legacy code areas actively modified with no tests present
Coverage percentage treated as a sign-off criterion; critical change paths untested
Tests assert on return values but not on important side effects (DB writes, events, state transitions)

sources.book: Feathers — Working Effectively with Legacy Code
sources.principle: Ch. 1: Legacy code is code without tests

sources.book: Osherove — The Art of Unit Testing

severity_guide.critical: legacy code area actively being modified with no tests; error-handling paths entirely absent
severity_guide.warning: coverage > 80% but edge and exception paths are systematically absent
severity_guide.suggestion: a few non-critical paths missing sad-path tests

what_not_to_flag: High line coverage is useful when paired with branch, boundary, and change-path coverage
A new module may have limited coverage early if it is still private and low-risk
Side-effect assertions may live in integration tests rather than unit tests without implying a gap

T6.name: Architecture Mismatch
T6.diagnostic: Does the test suite structure reflect the system's actual risk profile?
T6.consequence: Wrong suite shape is slow and expensive — not from bad tests, but from using the wrong type at the wrong layer.
T6.remedy: Rebalance the pyramid toward fast unit tests, introduce seams for isolation, and add characterization tests before modifying legacy areas (Feathers — Seam Model; Google — 70:20:10).
symptoms: Inverted test pyramid: E2E or integration test count exceeds unit test count
Legacy code with no seam points: no interfaces, DI, or seams to test in isolation
Legacy areas modified with no Characterization Tests to capture current behavior first
Full suite execution time exceeds 10 minutes (architectural, not performance, problem)
High-risk and low-risk paths tested at identical density; no risk-based prioritization

sources.book: Feathers — Working Effectively with Legacy Code
Feathers — Working Effectively with Legacy Code

severity_guide.critical: legacy code being modified has no seams and no characterization tests; pyramid fully inverted
severity_guide.warning: suite execution > 10 minutes; integration/E2E count exceeds unit tests
severity_guide.suggestion: localized pyramid ratio deviation; a few legacy areas missing characterization tests

what_not_to_flag: Deviating from 70:20:10 can be justified by platform constraints or product risk
A suite heavy on integration tests can still be healthy if feedback is fast and purposefully layered
A small number of critical-path E2E tests is desirable, not a smell
